paper.title: High-fiber diet durably reshapes the gut microbiome only when maintained: a 24-week washout randomized controlled trial
paper.abstract: We conducted a 24-week fiber intervention (n=200) with a 12-week washout phase to test whether microbiome changes persist after fiber cessation. High-fiber diet (35g/day) significantly increased Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Bifidobacterium longum, and Roseburia intestinalis abundance by week 12. Upon fiber cessation, these taxa returned to baseline within 3 weeks in 89% of participants. Diversity metrics (Shannon index) followed the same reversibility pattern. These findings confirm that dietary fiber effects on microbiome composition require sustained intake, supporting the 'diet-dependence' hypothesis of microbiome modulation.

classification.label: EXTENDS
classification.reason: Extends fnd_1 with human RCT evidence confirming that fiber-induced expansion of key taxa (F. prausnitzii, Roseburia) is reversible within 3 weeks of cessation, quantifying the maintenance requirement.

paper.title: Secondary bile acids produced by Clostridiales activate intestinal FXR to regulate host lipid metabolism and microbiome composition
paper.abstract: Gut bacteria of the Clostridiales order convert primary bile acids to secondary bile acids (deoxycholic acid, lithocholic acid) that activate the farnesoid X receptor (FXR) in intestinal epithelial cells. FXR activation induces FGF15/19, which suppresses hepatic bile acid synthesis and reshapes the microbiome toward bile-tolerant taxa. In germ-free mice colonized with Clostridium scindens, FXR activation reduced hepatic triglycerides by 31% and shifted microbiome composition toward Bacteroidetes. These findings identify a bile acid-FXR-FGF15 axis linking gut microbiome metabolism to host lipid homeostasis.

classification.label: EXTENDS
classification.reason: Extends oq_2 by identifying secondary bile acids as gut-derived microbial metabolites that mediate host metabolic signaling through FXR/FGF15, adding a bile acid axis to the metabolite-host communication framework.

paper.title: Longitudinal FMT engraftment dynamics reveal diet as the dominant force shaping post-transplant microbiome composition
paper.abstract: We tracked microbiome engraftment in 67 metabolic syndrome patients receiving lean donor FMT over 52 weeks. Donor-derived taxa achieved peak engraftment at week 4 but declined progressively, with 78% of patients returning to pre-FMT composition by week 24. Dietary fiber intake was the strongest predictor of sustained engraftment (β=0.68, p<0.001), explaining more variance than donor selection, delivery route, or antibiotic pre-treatment. These data confirm that diet shapes the engraftment niche and must accompany FMT for durable metabolic improvements.

classification.label: EXTENDS
classification.reason: Extends fnd_2 with 52-week longitudinal data showing diet explains 68% of FMT engraftment variance, quantifying the mechanism by which diet shapes the engraftment niche after lean-donor FMT.
